infoDialog.title: Information about the comparison
infoDialog.modeBody: In this mode you chat with two different AI models. Both models receive the same input and we want to find out, with your help, which model is better and simulates the client more effectively.
infoDialog.personaBody: Some details about the respective client are listed on the left sidebar.
infoDialog.ratingBody: After the AI models have written something, you should rate which of the two is better (or equally good). Then you can formulate a response to which the models respond again, and so on.
infoDialog.limitsBody: There is no limit here - you can write as much as you like. If you want to try another persona, simply switch to the next session using the bar below or the overview page.

empty.title: Import data

autoDetection.title: Auto-detection
autoDetection.body: Detects OpenAI, LMSYS, CSV, and more

wizardFlow.title: Wizard flow
wizardFlow.body: From upload to evaluation in one flow

overview.title: What is LLARS?
overview.body: {0} is a platform for collaborative evaluation of AI-generated content. The system enables systematic evaluation, comparison, and optimization of LLM responses in the context of e-counseling.

technical.title: Technical documentation
technical.body: The complete technical documentation with architecture, API reference, Agentic AI (RAG, ACT, ReACT, ReflAct), installation guides, and more can be found in our MkDocs documentation:

quickStart.title: Quick start
quickStart.code:
# Clone and start the repository
git clone <repository-url>
cd llars
cp .env.template.development .env
./start_llars.sh
quickStart.hint: After starting, LLARS is available at {0}.

live.emptyStart: Start the session to generate comparisons.

adminProgress.threadDetailsMeta: Thread ID: {threadId} | Chat ID: {chatId} | Institute ID: {instituteId}

controller.title: Who is responsible for data processing?
controller.intro: The party responsible for data protection on this website is:
controller.contact:
Sigrid Zauter, Managing Director of the Institute for E-Consulting
Keßlerplatz 12, 90489 Nuremberg
Tel.: 0911-5880-2580
Fax: 0911-5880-602580
Email: info [at] e-beratungsinstitut . de

dataCollection.title: What data is collected and why?
dataCollection.paragraph1: When you visit our website, some data is automatically collected, such as your IP address and the pages visited. This data helps us analyze website usage and improve it continuously.
dataCollection.paragraph2: In addition, we use data you voluntarily provide to evaluate it within the LLars research project. LLars is software developed to assess and analyze AI-generated content. All collected data is used exclusively for scientific research purposes and processed in anonymized form where possible.

recipients.title: Who receives my data?
recipients.paragraph1: Your data is used internally for research and development within the LLars project. It is not shared with third parties unless disclosure is legally required or you have given explicit consent.
recipients.paragraph2: The servers for this project are hosted on machines of the Nuremberg Institute of Technology (TH Nuremberg), and a data processing agreement has been concluded to ensure compliance with data protection.

protection.title: How is my data protected?
protection.paragraph1: We take measures to protect and securely store your data. This includes technical safeguards such as encryption and secure passwords.

rights.title: What are my access rights?
rights.paragraph1: You have the right to know what data we have stored about you, and you can request that this data be corrected or deleted. Contact us using the contact information above to exercise your rights.

changes.title: Changes to this privacy policy
changes.paragraph1: This privacy policy may be updated from time to time. Changes will be published on our website.

coherence.title: Coherence and logic
coherence.description: Do the responses follow a natural communication pattern?

overall.title: Overall rating
overall.description: Authentic and suitable as an example?

disclaimer.title: Disclaimer
disclaimer.text: The contents of this website and the information provided have been carefully researched and prepared to the best of our knowledge and belief. Nevertheless, the Nuremberg Institute of Technology cannot assume any liability for the timeliness, accuracy, completeness, legal permissibility, quality, and availability at all times of the information provided.

links.title: Links
links.text: Our own content must be distinguished from references ("links") to the websites of other providers. We cannot continuously check these external contents for changes and therefore cannot assume any responsibility for them. The respective provider of the page is solely liable for illegal, incorrect, or incomplete content and, in particular, for damages resulting from the use or non-use of information from third parties.

hero.title: OnCoCo 1.0
hero.subtitle: Online Counseling Conversations Dataset
hero.description: A fine-grained category and classification system for automated content analysis of psychosocial online counseling. Developed at TH Nuremberg using methods of qualitative social research.

hierarchy.title: The hierarchical category system
hierarchy.description: OnCoCo uses a 5-level hierarchical system to classify statements in online counseling sessions. Each category has a unique code that describes the path through the hierarchy.

impactFactors.title: Impact Factors - The core
impactFactors.alert: Impact Factors are the effectiveness-related categories according to Grawe (2000). They are the focus of the analysis because they reflect the quality and methodology of counseling.

items.name: Analysis & problem clarification
items.description: Capturing and understanding the problem through questions, reflections, and mirroring.

items.name: Goal setting
items.description: Defining and aligning counseling goals and expectations.

items.name: Motivation building
items.description: Promoting readiness for change, change talk (MI), appreciation.

items.name: Resource activation
items.description: Activating existing support from family, friends, or professional services.

items.name: Help & problem solving
items.description: Concrete recommendations, explanations, warnings, and advice.

quality.title: Quality monitoring
quality.subtitle: Make transitions and impact factors visible

training.title: Training & supervision
training.subtitle: Reveal patterns in conversations, provide feedback

research.title: Research
research.subtitle: Scalable content analysis for psychosocial counseling

chatbot.title: Chatbot evaluation
chatbot.subtitle: Compare humans vs. AI in fine-grained classes

model.title: Model & technology
model.roleMaskingValue: Output logits are filtered by role
model.warning: Zero-shot fails: GPT-4o reaches only F1 ~0.29 - fine-tuning is required!

items.text: To advise you better, it would be helpful to know what hobbies or interests you have.
items.path: Impact Factors → Analysis → Reflection → Living situation → Leisure

items.text: Can you describe the feeling you have afterwards a bit more precisely?
items.path: Impact Factors → Analysis → Reflection → Targeted inquiry

items.text: It is completely normal for young people in puberty to start developing sexual desires and fantasies.
It's actually more about my brother than about me.
I tried to ease my withdrawal symptoms with medication, but it feels like a constant struggle.
items.path: Impact Factors → Analysis → Feedback on previous attempts

clientItems.name: Disclosure of personal data
clientItems.description: Shares private data or background information.

clientItems.description: Asks generally for help or information.

clientItems.name: Goal of the assignment
clientItems.description: States the desired goal or outcome.

clientItems.name: Further use of professional resources

pillarComparison.title: Pairwise pillar comparisons
pillarComparison.empty: No comparison data available. Please wait until the analysis is complete.

controls.smoothingHint: Additive smoothing for zero counts. 0 = none, 1 = standard
controls.permutationsHint: Number of permutations for the significance test (more = more accurate, but slower)

chiSquare.label: Chi-square test
chiSquare.subtitle: States with significantly different transition distributions

missing.title: Missing transitions
missing.none: All transitions present in both matrices

items.frobenius: [1] Frobenius norm: Golub & Van Loan (2013). Matrix Computations. Johns Hopkins University Press.
items.jsd: [2] Jensen-Shannon divergence: Lin, J. (1991). Divergence measures based on the Shannon entropy. IEEE Transactions on Information Theory.
items.permutation: [3] Permutation test: Vautard, R. et al. (1990). Monte Carlo and parametric tests. Journal of Statistical Planning and Inference.
items.effectSize: [4] Effect size: Cohen, J. (1988). Statistical Power Analysis for the Behavioral Sciences. Routledge.
items.chiSquare: [5] Chi-square for Markov chains: Anderson, T.W. & Goodman, L.A. (1957). Statistical inference about Markov chains. The Annals of Mathematical Statistics.

frobenius.title: Frobenius distance
frobenius.whatIs: The Frobenius norm measures the overall difference between two matrices as the Euclidean distance of all matrix elements.
frobenius.exampleIntro: If we compare the transition matrices of pillar 1 (role plays) and pillar 3 (anonymized data):

examples.item1: A Frobenius distance of 0.1 means the counseling patterns are very similar
examples.item2: A distance of 0.5 shows clear differences in communication transitions
examples.item3: The larger the value, the more differently counselors behave across the data sources

frobenius.source: Golub, G.H. & Van Loan, C.F. (2013). Matrix Computations (4th ed.). Johns Hopkins University Press.

jsd.title: Jensen-Shannon divergence (JSD)
jsd.whatIs: JSD is a symmetric measure of the difference between two probability distributions. It is based on Kullback-Leibler divergence, but symmetric and always defined.
jsd.exampleIntro: For each row of the transition matrix (each source state) we compute the JSD:

examples.item1: If a counselor after an "Active listening" intervention (CO-IF-AC) in pillar 1 usually moves to "Questions" but in pillar 3 to "Informing", the JSD for this row shows a high value
examples.item2: Average JSD near 0: the transition patterns are almost identical
examples.item3: Average JSD near 1: the transition patterns are completely different

jsd.source: Lin, J. (1991). Divergence measures based on the Shannon entropy. IEEE Transactions on Information Theory, 37(1), 145-151.

permutation.title: Permutation test
permutation.whatIs: A non-parametric significance test that checks the null hypothesis that two matrices come from the same distribution.

steps.step1: Compute the observed Frobenius distance between the original matrices
steps.step2: Randomly mix the count data of both matrices
steps.step3: Compute the Frobenius distance of the mixed matrices
steps.step5: p-value = proportion of permutations with distance >= observed distance

permutation.exampleIntro: We test whether the differences between role plays (pillar 1) and real counseling sessions (pillar 3) are statistically significant:

examples.item1: p < 0.05: The differences are significant - role plays do not perfectly mirror real counseling
examples.item2: p >= 0.05: No significant difference detected - the data sources may show the same patterns

permutation.source: Good, P. (2005). Permutation, Parametric and Bootstrap Tests of Hypotheses (3rd ed.). Springer.

effect.title: Effect size
effect.whatIs: Effect size indicates how large a difference is in practice, independent of sample size. A significant difference can be small when the sample is very large.
effect.normalizedNote: where n, m are the matrix dimensions
effect.exampleIntro: Even if the permutation test is significant (p < 0.05), the effect size shows whether the difference is practically relevant:

examples.item1: Small effect size (< 0.2): The differences are statistical but hardly relevant in practice - counselors behave similarly
examples.item2: Medium effect size (0.2 - 0.5): Noticeable differences in counseling patterns
examples.item3: Large effect size (> 0.5): Clearly different counseling styles between the data sources

effect.source: Cohen, J. (1988). Statistical Power Analysis for the Behavioral Sciences (2nd ed.). Routledge.

chiSquare.title: Chi-square test for Markov chains
chiSquare.whatIs: The chi-square test checks for each source state whether the transition probabilities differ significantly.
chiSquare.formulaNote: where O = observed, E = expected frequencies
chiSquare.exampleIntro: For each counseling code (e.g. CO-IF-AC "Active listening") we test separately:

examples.item1: If after "Active listening" in pillar 1 60% move to "Questions" but in pillar 3 only 40%, the chi-square test shows whether this difference is significant
examples.item2: This shows exactly which counseling techniques differ between data sources

chiSquare.interpretationIntro: The share of significant states (p < 0.05) shows how many counseling techniques differ:
chiSquare.source: Anderson, T.W. & Goodman, L.A. (1957). Statistical inference about Markov chains. The Annals of Mathematical Statistics, 28(1), 89-110.

laplace.title: Laplace smoothing (additive smoothing)
laplace.whatIs: Laplace smoothing prevents issues with zero counts in the matrix by adding a small value to all cells.
laplace.exampleIntro: If a specific transition (e.g. "Questions" -> "Confrontation") never occurs in pillar 1 but occurs 5 times in pillar 3:

examples.item1: Without smoothing: division by zero possible, JSD = infinity
examples.item2: With alpha = 1: each cell gets at least 1 pseudo-count
examples.item3: Higher alpha: more conservative estimate but smooths real differences

recommended.title: Recommended values
recommended.alphaZero: alpha = 0: No smoothing (only with complete data)

laplace.source: Manning, C.D., Raghavan, P. & Schuetze, H. (2008). Introduction to Information Retrieval. Cambridge University Press.

Anna Müller

connection.title: Zotero connection
connection.prompt: Connect your Zotero account to sync libraries and citations.

connect.oauthHint: Sign in with Zotero to connect securely.
connect.apiKeyHint: Enter a Zotero API key to connect.
connect.apiKeyHintAlt: Use an API key instead of OAuth.

dialogs.removeHint: This will unlink the library but won't delete it from Zotero.

home.title: LaTeX Collab AI
home.subtitle: Collaborative LaTeX workspace with AI writing assistance.

banner.title: AI features enabled
banner.body: Includes Ghost Text, AI commands, the AI sidebar, and RAG citations.

dialogs.leaveHint: You can be invited again by the owner.

sidebar.title: AI Assistant

welcome.greeting: Hi! I'm your AI writing assistant.

sidebar.footerTip: Tip: Use AI commands in the editor for quick actions.

commandDescriptions.ai: Ask anything or give an instruction.
commandDescriptions.rewrite: Rewrite the selected text in a more academic style.
commandDescriptions.expand: Expand the selected text with more detail.
commandDescriptions.translate: Translate the text to a target language.
commandDescriptions.cite: Find suitable sources for the statement.
commandDescriptions.abstract: Generate an abstract for the document.

rollback.title: Discard changes?
rollback.forceConfirm: This will discard local changes for {path}. Continue?
rollback.simpleConfirm: All uncommitted changes will be discarded and the document will reset to the last commit.
